requirements_for_qualification.summary: Must have earned income below phase-out thresholds to directly contribute to Roth IRA, building tax-free retirement savings with no required minimum distributions and tax-free qualified withdrawals in retirement
criteria: Must have earned income (wages, self-employment, alimony from pre-2019 divorces) to contribute
2024 contribution limit: $7,000 under age 50; $8,000 age 50+ (indexed annually)
Contributions not deductible but grow tax-free and qualified distributions tax-free
Contribution can be made until tax filing deadline (April 15) for prior tax year
No age limit for contributions as long as have earned income (unlike pre-2020 traditional IRA rules)
Can withdraw contributions (not earnings) anytime tax and penalty-free (contributions already taxed)
Qualified distributions (age 59½ + 5-year rule) completely tax-free including all earnings

roth_ira_contribution_rules.tax_free_growth: Contributions grow tax-free; qualified distributions completely tax-free including earnings
roth_ira_contribution_rules.contribution_flexibility: Can withdraw contributions anytime without tax or penalty (not earnings)
roth_ira_contribution_rules.no_rmds: No required minimum distributions during owner's lifetime; ideal for legacy planning
roth_ira_contribution_rules.spousal_contribution: Non-working spouse can contribute based on working spouse's earned income
roth_ira_contribution_rules.five_year_rule: Account must be open 5+ years for earnings to be tax-free (even if over age 59½)

restrictions: Income phase-out reduces or eliminates contribution ability; use backdoor Roth if over limits
Cannot contribute more than earned income for the year; $7,000/$8,000 or earned income, whichever less
Contribution limit combined with traditional IRA; cannot contribute $7,000 to each (total limit applies)
Early withdrawal of earnings (before age 59½ and 5 years) subject to income tax and 10% penalty
Excess contributions subject to 6% excise tax annually until corrected; must withdraw excess with earnings
Recharacterization of current-year Roth to traditional allowed; conversion recharacterization eliminated after 2017
Modified AGI calculation for phase-out different from regular AGI; certain income excluded/added
State tax treatment uniformly follows federal; Roth contributions and distributions tax-free at state level
